title: Carved beech mustard spoon
subtitle: A small treen mustard spoon for beginner carvers
excerpt: A small beech mustard spoon; the ideal first spoon-carving project. The bowl is tiny, the handle is short, and the whole piece fits in one hand while you carve it. Beech works cleanly and is widely available. This takes an afternoon from a fresh billet.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for small treen forms; the mustard spoon as a recognised treen type is documented in museum collections at the Victoria and Albert Museum.

content.text: The mustard spoon is
treen

at its most compact: 12 to 14 cm overall, with a round or oval bowl the width of a thumb-tip. It sits in a mustard pot, jams into a salt cellar, spoons honey from a jar. The small scale makes it the natural first spoon project: less waste to remove, less hook-knife work, and a mistake that goes too thin is visible sooner. Beech is the wood; it carves cleanly
green
, dries pale and close-grained, and is food-safe without any treatment beyond linseed oil.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one; the dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood
A small
blank

from green beech: 16 cm long, 5 cm wide, 3 cm deep. The piece is small enough that a short offcut from a larger beech log will do. Beech is common in hedgerows and woodland across southern England and much of Europe; windfall and pruning offcuts are reliable sources. Alternatively a small split billet from the firewood pile; beech is a standard firewood species.
Tools

A sloyd knife and a small hook knife are the only cutting tools. At this scale, an axe is unnecessary; the sloyd knife does all the rough shaping. A leather strop and raw linseed oil complete the kit.
Method
Draw the spoon profile on the flat face of the blank: handle 9 cm, neck, bowl oval 3 cm long and 2.5 cm wide. Mark the bowl depth on the side face; 1 cm below the flat face at the deepest point.
Remove the waste around the profile with
push cuts
on the sloyd knife: four-finger grip on the blank, blade travelling away from the body. Work to within 3 mm of the pencil line.
Refine the handle and neck with
thumb-pivot cuts
: thumb of the off-hand pressing on the back of the blade, blade pivoting in short arcs. This gives tight control around the neck and the transition into the bowl.

Hollow the bowl with the small hook knife. At this scale the bowl is shallow; aim for 8 mm depth at most. Grip: bowl face up, hook knife in the cutting hand, thumb of the holding hand braced against the back of the blade. Shavings work from rim toward the centre with the grain.

Check the wall thickness by holding the spoon to the light. At this small scale, walls should be no less than 2 mm. Thin walls at the rim of a mustard spoon are the commonest failure; carve the back of the bowl to match the front hollow rather than treating them separately.
Leave to dry 1 to 2 weeks before finishing. The small mass dries faster than a full eating spoon.
Finishing

Sand with 120 then 180 grit, following the grain. A small strip of sandpaper wrapped around a pencil helps reach inside the bowl. Apply raw linseed oil; leave for an hour; wipe off the surplus. Cure time is 2 weeks before food contact.
Variations
Honey spoon: the same profile with the bowl reduced to a narrow oval; matched to a honey jar opening.
Sycamore: the same method. Sycamore is the traditional English treen wood and dries slightly paler than beech.
Left-handed version: invert the neck angle slightly so the bowl sits naturally in the non-dominant hand for scooping.
Care
Wash with warm water after each use. Towel-dry at once. Re-oil when the wood looks dry. Never dishwasher or long soak.

items.fix: Work the hook knife from the rim inward, always finishing the stroke before reaching the far rim. Check depth with a pencil tick rather than cutting by feel.
items.cause: Hook knife came over the rim rather than staying within the bowl oval
items.symptom: Bowl walls go thin before reaching target depth

items.fix: Leave the neck slightly thick until the bowl is hollowed. The neck is the last thing to refine, not the first.
items.cause: Neck carved too narrow before the bowl was hollowed; the stress of the hook-knife work cracked it

items.fix: Read the grain before each cut: the grain lines on the side of the blank show which direction the fibres run. Shorten the cut length and check direction after every two or three strokes.
items.cause: Grain running at an angle to the blank face
items.symptom: Sloyd knife drifts off the pencil line on push cuts
